title: Backtracking
description: Recursion with a choose/explore/unchoose pattern: generating every subset by trying a decision, exploring deeper, then undoing it to try the next.
objectives: Understand backtracking as recursion plus explicit undoing
Generate all subsets of a set using backtracking
Understand why the 'unchoose' step is essential

sections.text: Recursion in DSA built functions that call themselves toward a base case. Backtracking adds one more idea on top: trying a choice, exploring where it leads, and then explicitly undoing that choice to try the next alternative.
Backtracking explores all possible choices by recursively trying one option at a time, undoing ("backtracking") each choice after exploring it, so the next alternative can be tried cleanly.

sections.title: How it explores every combination
points: For each element, there are exactly two choices: include it, or don't
The function tries BOTH choices recursively for every element — that's 2 × 2 × 2 = 8 total combinations for 3 elements
current.push_back(...) is the CHOOSE step; current.pop_back() is the UNCHOOSE step, restoring current to how it was before trying that branch
Without pop_back(), current would keep accumulating leftover elements from earlier branches, corrupting every later result

sections.text: The name comes directly from that undo step: after fully exploring a choice, the algorithm literally "backtracks" — undoes the decision — to return to the state it was in before, so the next possibility can be explored from a clean slate. Forgetting to undo is the single most common backtracking bug, since it silently corrupts every subsequent branch's results.

sections.title: Trying on outfits and putting each item back before trying the next combination
sections.text: Trying every possible outfit combination means putting on an item, seeing the full look, and then taking that specific item back off before trying the next option — not leaving it on and piling more clothes on top. Piling on top of previous choices without undoing them would corrupt every future combination you tried to explore.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Forgetting the 'unchoose' step (like pop_back()) — leftover state from a previous branch corrupts all following results.
Modifying shared state without a plan to undo it — every CHOOSE needs a matching UNCHOOSE, symmetric to how it was applied.
Confusing backtracking with plain recursion — backtracking specifically implies exploring multiple choices at each step and reverting between them, not just a single recursive descent.
Not recognizing the exponential nature of trying every choice at every step — generating all subsets of n elements is inherently O(2ⁿ), matching the branching we saw in naive Fibonacci.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is backtracking?
questions.answer: A technique that explores all possible choices by recursively trying one option, exploring where it leads, and then undoing that choice before trying the next alternative.

questions.question: What are the three steps of the backtracking pattern?
questions.answer: Choose (make a decision), explore (recurse with that decision in effect), and unchoose (undo the decision before trying the next option).

questions.question: Why is the 'unchoose' step essential in backtracking?
questions.answer: Without undoing a choice after exploring it, leftover state carries into later branches, corrupting the results of every subsequent combination explored.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of generating all subsets of an n-element set?
questions.answer: O(2ⁿ), since each element independently has two choices (include or exclude), giving 2ⁿ total combinations to explore.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Backtracking builds on recursion by exploring every possible choice at each step: choose an option, recursively explore what follows from it, then unchoose — undo that option — before trying the next alternative. Generating all subsets demonstrates this cleanly: for each element, recursively try both excluding and including it, using push_back() to choose and pop_back() to unchoose after exploring. The undo step is what gives backtracking its name and is essential — skipping it lets leftover state from one branch silently corrupt the results of every branch explored afterward. This exhaustive choice-at-every-step approach is inherently O(2ⁿ) for problems like generating subsets.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Backtracking
sections.text: Backtracking = try on an outfit, explore the look, take it back OFF before trying the next combo. Choose → Explore → Unchoose. Forgetting to unchoose corrupts everything after.

sections.title: Subsets
sections.problemStatement: Given an array nums of unique integers, return all possible subsets (the power set).
hints: This is exactly this lesson's own example — collect every 'current' combination at every leaf of the exclude/include recursion tree.
At each element, recursively explore both NOT including it and including it.
Remember to push_back() the current subset's copy into the results whenever a leaf is reached, and pop_back() when unwinding to try the next branch.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Use bitmasking: every number from 0 to 2ⁿ-1 represents one subset, where bit i decides whether nums[i] is included.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: This lesson's own exclude/include backtracking, collecting the current combination at every leaf.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n × 2ⁿ) for the output, O(n) call stack
